user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.35,00,000/- being the Plaintiff's share in a dissolved partnership firm. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in M/s ABC Enterprises carrying on business of construction materials. The partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024. Upon dissolution, accounts were settled and the Defendant acknowledged owing Rs.35,00,000/- to the Plaintiff as his share. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has not paid. Plead under Sections 48 and 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 and Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Claim interest at 12% per annum from the date of dissolution. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation and verification. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 35,00,000 as their share from a dissolved partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises. The partnership was dissolved on 01.06.2024, and the Defendant acknowledged the debt but has failed to pay despite demands.

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Between Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, this action for money recovery is instituted. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff commenced this action by filing the plaint against the Defendant in {{JURISDICTION}}. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue; the Defendant is sui juris and competent to be sued.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
Territorial jurisdiction: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, {{STATE}}, within the local limits of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}. The advance payment was made by the Plaintiff from its account at {{BANK_NAME}} Bank, {{BANK_BRANCH}}, which is situated within the same local limits. The partnership agreement between the parties was executed at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, which lies within the said territorial frame. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 when the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent within the territorial reach of this Court, and the Defendant acknowledged the debt but has not paid despite demands.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks Rs. 35,00,000 as the principal amount due, with interest at 12% per annum on the outstanding amount. The total value of the claim, including interest up to the date of filing, remains within the monetary limits of this District Court.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises, and their relationship was formalized by a partnership agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A). By mutual consent, the partnership was dissolved on 01.06.2024, with the parties intending to settle all liabilities and distribute the partnership assets as per the terms of the agreement reflected in Annexure A. 

2. In contemplation of the dissolution, the parties agreed that the Plaintiff would receive Rs 3,500,000 as his share of the dissolved firm’s assets and liabilities, a sum which the Plaintiff contends was otherwise due to him from the Defendant. Annexure A records the dissolution arrangement and the understanding that such sums would be payable to the Plaintiff; the Plaintiff further contends that interest would accrue on any unpaid amount at the rate of 12% per annum as provided in the terms of the arrangement. 

3. Under the dissolution agreement, the Defendant undertook to pay Rs 3,500,000 to the Plaintiff and to discharge his share by the performance date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, with any delay in payment to accrue interest at 12% per annum from the due date forward, in accordance with Annexure A. The Plaintiff relied on this undertaking as the Defendant’s binding obligation arising out of the partnership dissolution. 

4. The Plaintiff did not receive any payment from the Defendant in respect of the Rs 3,500,000 claim either on or after 01.06.2024 or up to the date of filing, as evidenced by Annexure B, which contains the payment proof and shows no completed payment to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff’s claim for the amount due is Rs 3,500,000, with the agreed 12% per annum interest on any overdue amount, and Annexure B is relied upon to confirm the absence of a completed payment. 

5. The specific default consists of the Defendant’s failure to settle the amount of Rs 3,500,000 by the performance date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} despite the Defendant’s acknowledgment of the debt and the running obligation to pay, as evidenced by Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document). The lack of payment persists notwithstanding the Plaintiff’s repeated reminders, and the obligation remains unsatisfied as of the date of filing this suit. 

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding payment of Rs 3,500,000 along with accrued interest, dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and served on the Defendant, as detailed in Annexure C. The Defendant’s response, if any, is contained in Annexure D, dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, wherein the Defendant addressed the notice but did not offer to settle the debt. The Plaintiff subsequently filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, and the matter is supported by Annexure F and other accompanying documents, including Annexure E (acknowledgment) and Annexure C (notice), to establish the sequence of events and the continuing cause of action. The cause of action first arose on 01.06.2024, the date of dissolution.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff relies on Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as the primary legal basis for recovery. Section 65 creates a restitutionary obligation: when an agreement is found void or when a valid contract subsequently becomes void, any benefit or advantage received by one party under such agreement must be restored to the person from whom it was received and the law imposes a duty to compensate the aggrieved party for the loss caused by the retention of such benefit. In the present case, the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, and the Plaintiff is entitled to his share in the dissolved assets, quantified at Rs. 3,500,000, for which the Defendant acknowledged indebtedness but has refused or failed to pay despite demands communicated around the date of dissolution. The Defendant’s retention of Rs. 3,500,000 constitutes a benefit retained under the dissolved partnership arrangement with no lawful justification, and there has been no corresponding performance or consideration rendered by the Plaintiff after dissolution; in other words, there is a clear failure of consideration, since no further value, asset, or service was exchanged to justify the Defendant’s continued possession of the Plaintiff’s funds. The Defendant’s non-payment despite a formal legal notice (Annexure C) exacerbates the breach and triggers the restitutionary remedy under Section 65, which obligates the Defendant to restore the Plaintiff’s amount in full along with interest at 12% per annum from the date of dissolution, i.e., 01.06.2024, until payment. The relief sought is, therefore, restitution of Rs. 3,500,000 and accrued interest, framed as refund/restitution to place the Plaintiff in his pre-dissolution position. The Plaintiff has not raised any counter-claim or set-off, and no forfeiture clause exists in the dissolution documents (Annexure A), nor has any valid justification been advanced by the Defendant to bar restitution; demands were duly served through Annexure C. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court determines that the underlying dissolution does not render the contract void in the sense contemplated by Section 65, the Plaintiff maintains that the debt remains payable as a liquidated claim arising from the dissolved partnership, and the Defendant remains liable to restore the Plaintiff’s share under the same restitutionary principle, along with interest as described. The defensive arguments the Defendant is likely to raise—such as no part performance, forfeiture, adjustment, or counter-claim—are not supported by the facts: a formal dissolution on 01.06.2024, a clear acknowledgment of debt by the Defendant, and lawful notices establishing the Plaintiff’s entitlement to recovery, all of which negate any justification to withhold payment. Annexure A and Annexure C are relied upon to establish the existence of the agreement/partnership framework and the demand for payment, respectively.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 01.06.2024, when the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent and the Defendant acknowledged the debt owed to the Plaintiff in respect of the Plaintiff's share in the dissolved firm, amounting to Rs. 35,00,000, thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for payment. The cause of action further accrued thereafter as the Defendant, having acknowledged the debt, repeatedly failed and refused to repay the said sum despite the Plaintiff's demands, and the outstanding amount, together with interest at 12.0% per annum, continued to accrue from 01.06.2024. The cause of action is continuing because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 35,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the Plaintiff's claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. The limitation period applicable to the plaintiff's claim commences from the cause of action accrual on 01.06.2024, the date the partnership was dissolved and the debt became recoverable, and runs for three years, terminating on {{DATE}}. The plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} in the appropriate court, which is before {{DATE}}. The action is timely and within the permitted period.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, filed this plaint in {{COURT_NAME}} against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, for recovery of Rs 3,500,000 as principal with interest at 12% per annum. The suit valuation is equal to the claim amount, being the principal sum of Rs 3,500,000 together with the claimed interest. The court fee payable for this suit has been calculated in accordance with the applicable state court fees act and is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the date of default ({{DEFAULT_DATE}}) until the filing of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}). The facts establish that the parties entered into a contract on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} under which the Plaintiff advanced Rs. 3,500,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and, by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the principal due. The funds remained with the Defendant, amounting to wrongful retention in a commercial transaction, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The rate of 12.0% per annum is justified because the transaction is commercial in nature and the parties had a contractual understanding that the sums would be repaid; charging a commercial rate compensates for the use of funds and the opportunity cost suffered by the Plaintiff during the pre-suit period.

The Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the filing date ({{FILING_DATE}}) to the end of the pendency of the suit ({{PENDENTE_LITE_END_DATE}}) under Section 34 CPC. The legal basis is Section 34 CPC, which authorizes interest on a money decree for the period the suit remains undecided. The facts show that the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}; the Defendant has benefited from the use of the Plaintiff's money during the pendency of the proceedings, and the Plaintiff has been deprived of the use of its funds during this period. A 12.0% per annum rate is warranted to reflect the commercial nature of the transaction and the contractual understanding, ensuring fair compensation for the Plaintiff during litigation.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the date of decree ({{DECREED_DATE}}) until realization ({{REALIZATION_DATE}}). The justification lies in the commercial character of the underlying transaction and the existence of a contractual understanding between the parties; post-decree interest is appropriate to secure full restitution and aligns with prevailing mercantile practice and rate expectations in banking norms, ensuring the Plaintiff is compensated for delayed realization of the judgment.

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

Between Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, this action for money recovery is instituted. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff commenced this action by filing the plaint against the Defendant in {{JURISDICTION}}. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue; the Defendant is sui juris and competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

Territorial jurisdiction: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, {{STATE}}, within the local limits of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}. The advance payment was made by the Plaintiff from its account at {{BANK_NAME}} Bank, {{BANK_BRANCH}}, which is situated within the same local limits. The partnership agreement between the parties was executed at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, which lies within the said territorial frame. The cause of action arose on 01.06.2024 when the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent within the territorial reach of this Court, and the Defendant acknowledged the debt but has not paid despite demands.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks Rs. 35,00,000 as the principal amount due, with interest at 12% per annum on the outstanding amount. The total value of the claim, including interest up to the date of filing, remains within the monetary limits of this District Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and Defendant were partners in the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises, and their relationship was formalized by a partnership agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A). By mutual consent, the partnership was dissolved on 01.06.2024, with the parties intending to settle all liabilities and distribute the partnership assets as per the terms of the agreement reflected in Annexure A. 

2. In contemplation of the dissolution, the parties agreed that the Plaintiff would receive Rs 3,500,000 as his share of the dissolved firm’s assets and liabilities, a sum which the Plaintiff contends was otherwise due to him from the Defendant. Annexure A records the dissolution arrangement and the understanding that such sums would be payable to the Plaintiff; the Plaintiff further contends that interest would accrue on any unpaid amount at the rate of 12% per annum as provided in the terms of the arrangement. 

3. Under the dissolution agreement, the Defendant undertook to pay Rs 3,500,000 to the Plaintiff and to discharge his share by the performance date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, with any delay in payment to accrue interest at 12% per annum from the due date forward, in accordance with Annexure A. The Plaintiff relied on this undertaking as the Defendant’s binding obligation arising out of the partnership dissolution. 

4. The Plaintiff did not receive any payment from the Defendant in respect of the Rs 3,500,000 claim either on or after 01.06.2024 or up to the date of filing, as evidenced by Annexure B, which contains the payment proof and shows no completed payment to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff’s claim for the amount due is Rs 3,500,000, with the agreed 12% per annum interest on any overdue amount, and Annexure B is relied upon to confirm the absence of a completed payment. 

5. The specific default consists of the Defendant’s failure to settle the amount of Rs 3,500,000 by the performance date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} despite the Defendant’s acknowledgment of the debt and the running obligation to pay, as evidenced by Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document). The lack of payment persists notwithstanding the Plaintiff’s repeated reminders, and the obligation remains unsatisfied as of the date of filing this suit. 

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding payment of Rs 3,500,000 along with accrued interest, dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and served on the Defendant, as detailed in Annexure C. The Defendant’s response, if any, is contained in Annexure D, dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, wherein the Defendant addressed the notice but did not offer to settle the debt. The Plaintiff subsequently filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, and the matter is supported by Annexure F and other accompanying documents, including Annexure E (acknowledgment) and Annexure C (notice), to establish the sequence of events and the continuing cause of action. The cause of action first arose on 01.06.2024, the date of dissolution.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

The Plaintiff relies on Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as the primary legal basis for recovery. Section 65 creates a restitutionary obligation: when an agreement is found void or when a valid contract subsequently becomes void, any benefit or advantage received by one party under such agreement must be restored to the person from whom it was received and the law imposes a duty to compensate the aggrieved party for the loss caused by the retention of such benefit. In the present case, the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024, and the Plaintiff is entitled to his share in the dissolved assets, quantified at Rs. 3,500,000, for which the Defendant acknowledged indebtedness but has refused or failed to pay despite demands communicated around the date of dissolution. The Defendant’s retention of Rs. 3,500,000 constitutes a benefit retained under the dissolved partnership arrangement with no lawful justification, and there has been no corresponding performance or consideration rendered by the Plaintiff after dissolution; in other words, there is a clear failure of consideration, since no further value, asset, or service was exchanged to justify the Defendant’s continued possession of the Plaintiff’s funds. The Defendant’s non-payment despite a formal legal notice (Annexure C) exacerbates the breach and triggers the restitutionary remedy under Section 65, which obligates the Defendant to restore the Plaintiff’s amount in full along with interest at 12% per annum from the date of dissolution, i.e., 01.06.2024, until payment. The relief sought is, therefore, restitution of Rs. 3,500,000 and accrued interest, framed as refund/restitution to place the Plaintiff in his pre-dissolution position. The Plaintiff has not raised any counter-claim or set-off, and no forfeiture clause exists in the dissolution documents (Annexure A), nor has any valid justification been advanced by the Defendant to bar restitution; demands were duly served through Annexure C. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court determines that the underlying dissolution does not render the contract void in the sense contemplated by Section 65, the Plaintiff maintains that the debt remains payable as a liquidated claim arising from the dissolved partnership, and the Defendant remains liable to restore the Plaintiff’s share under the same restitutionary principle, along with interest as described. The defensive arguments the Defendant is likely to raise—such as no part performance, forfeiture, adjustment, or counter-claim—are not supported by the facts: a formal dissolution on 01.06.2024, a clear acknowledgment of debt by the Defendant, and lawful notices establishing the Plaintiff’s entitlement to recovery, all of which negate any justification to withhold payment. Annexure A and Annexure C are relied upon to establish the existence of the agreement/partnership framework and the demand for payment, respectively.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 01.06.2024, when the partnership firm M/s ABC Enterprises was dissolved by mutual consent and the Defendant acknowledged the debt owed to the Plaintiff in respect of the Plaintiff's share in the dissolved firm, amounting to Rs. 35,00,000, thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for payment. The cause of action further accrued thereafter as the Defendant, having acknowledged the debt, repeatedly failed and refused to repay the said sum despite the Plaintiff's demands, and the outstanding amount, together with interest at 12.0% per annum, continued to accrue from 01.06.2024. The cause of action is continuing because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 35,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the Plaintiff's claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization.


LIMITATION

The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. The limitation period applicable to the plaintiff's claim commences from the cause of action accrual on 01.06.2024, the date the partnership was dissolved and the debt became recoverable, and runs for three years, terminating on {{DATE}}. The plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} in the appropriate court, which is before {{DATE}}. The action is timely and within the permitted period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, filed this plaint in {{COURT_NAME}} against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, for recovery of Rs 3,500,000 as principal with interest at 12% per annum. The suit valuation is equal to the claim amount, being the principal sum of Rs 3,500,000 together with the claimed interest. The court fee payable for this suit has been calculated in accordance with the applicable state court fees act and is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the date of default ({{DEFAULT_DATE}}) until the filing of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}). The facts establish that the parties entered into a contract on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} under which the Plaintiff advanced Rs. 3,500,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and, by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the principal due. The funds remained with the Defendant, amounting to wrongful retention in a commercial transaction, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The rate of 12.0% per annum is justified because the transaction is commercial in nature and the parties had a contractual understanding that the sums would be repaid; charging a commercial rate compensates for the use of funds and the opportunity cost suffered by the Plaintiff during the pre-suit period.

The Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the filing date ({{FILING_DATE}}) to the end of the pendency of the suit ({{PENDENTE_LITE_END_DATE}}) under Section 34 CPC. The legal basis is Section 34 CPC, which authorizes interest on a money decree for the period the suit remains undecided. The facts show that the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}; the Defendant has benefited from the use of the Plaintiff's money during the pendency of the proceedings, and the Plaintiff has been deprived of the use of its funds during this period. A 12.0% per annum rate is warranted to reflect the commercial nature of the transaction and the contractual understanding, ensuring fair compensation for the Plaintiff during litigation.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest on Rs. 3,500,000 at 12.0% per annum from the date of decree ({{DECREED_DATE}}) until realization ({{REALIZATION_DATE}}). The justification lies in the commercial character of the underlying transaction and the existence of a contractual understanding between the parties; post-decree interest is appropriate to secure full restitution and aligns with prevailing mercantile practice and rate expectations in banking norms, ensuring the Plaintiff is compensated for delayed realization of the judgment.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff the principal sum of Rs. 3,500,000/- (Rupees Three Million Five Hundred Thousand Only) being the amount due on account of the dissolved partnership as on 01.06.2024, despite demand. 
(b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of 12.0% per annum on the said principal sum from 01.06.2024 to the date of filing of the present suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) In terms of Section 34 CPC, award pendente lite interest at the rate of 12.0% per annum on the said principal sum from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree. 
(d) Award future interest at the rate of 12.0% per annum on the principal sum from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit including the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges amounting to Rs. {{COSTS_AMOUNT}}/-. 
(f) Such other relief as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of this case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

7. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure A
8. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure B
9. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure C
10. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure D
11. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure E
12. {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure F


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only)
WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN:

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
{{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.  
...Plaintiff

VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
{{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.  
...Defendant


THE PLAINT

1. The Plaintiff is a major and is competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is a major and is competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

2. Territorial Jurisdiction: This Hon’ble Court has territorial jurisdiction since (i) the Defendant resides and carries on business within the local limits of this Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, and (ii) the partnership business of M/s ABC Enterprises was carried on at {{FIRM_PRINCIPAL_PLACE_OF_BUSINESS}} within the local limits of this Court, and (iii) the dissolution/settlement between the parties and the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability were made at {{DISSOLUTION_SETTLEMENT_PLACE}} within the local limits of this Court, and (iv) the amount claimed is payable to the Plaintiff at {{PAYMENT_PLACE}} within the local limits of this Court.

3. Pecuniary Jurisdiction: The suit claim is for recovery of Rs. 35,00,000/- along with interest. The suit valuation for purposes of jurisdiction is stated in the Valuation paragraph below and is within the pecuniary limits of this Hon’ble District Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE (CHRONOLOGICAL)

4. The Plaintiff and the Defendant were partners in a partnership firm in the name and style of “M/s ABC Enterprises”, carrying on the business of construction materials, inter alia at {{FIRM_PRINCIPAL_PLACE_OF_BUSINESS}}. The partnership commenced on {{PARTNERSHIP_START_DATE}}. The partnership deed, if any, is relied upon as Annexure A.

5. Due to mutual differences/for business reasons, the Plaintiff and Defendant mutually agreed to dissolve the partnership firm. Accordingly, the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024 (hereinafter “Date of Dissolution”). The dissolution deed/settlement memo, if reduced into writing, is relied upon as Annexure B.

6. Upon dissolution, the partners settled the accounts of the firm and agreed the manner of distribution of the assets and liabilities of the dissolved firm. In such settlement, the Defendant acknowledged that a sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) is due and payable by the Defendant to the Plaintiff towards the Plaintiff’s share in the dissolved partnership firm. The written acknowledgment/settlement record (if separate) is relied upon as Annexure C.

7. After 01.06.2024, the Plaintiff repeatedly demanded payment of the said sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- from the Defendant, including on {{DEMAND_DATE_1}}, {{DEMAND_DATE_2}} and thereafter. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has failed and neglected to pay the Plaintiff the said sum and continues to wrongfully withhold the Plaintiff’s admitted share.

8. The Defendant’s liability is thus a liquidated sum arising out of the settlement of accounts upon dissolution and the Defendant’s acknowledgment thereof, but remains unpaid till date.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

9. Section 48 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 (user-instructed) sets out the mode of settlement of accounts between partners after dissolution, including adjustment of losses and distribution/application of the assets and surplus among the partners in their respective rights. In the present case, the partnership stood dissolved on 01.06.2024 and the accounts were settled, resulting in an admitted balance of Rs. 35,00,000/- payable to the Plaintiff as his share. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to recover the said admitted sum from the Defendant.

10. Section 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 (user-instructed) (a) does not bar the present suit because {{FIRM_REGISTRATION_STATUS_AND_PARTNERS_SHOWN}}. Without prejudice, even otherwise, the Plaintiff’s claim is founded upon settlement of accounts of a dissolved partnership and a post-dissolution acknowledgment/arrangement fixing the amount payable, and is maintainable under the applicable exception(s) under the Partnership Act including Section 69(3)(a), as applicable to the facts.

11. Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (user-instructed) is pleaded without prejudice and in the alternative: if this Hon’ble Court holds that the post-dissolution settlement/arrangement recording the Plaintiff’s entitlement is unenforceable/void for any reason, then the Defendant—having retained and continued to retain the monetary benefit representing the Plaintiff’s admitted share—must restore/return the benefit so retained to the Plaintiff.

12. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest. The Plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 as specifically instructed, on account of the Defendant’s wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s admitted share after dissolution/settlement, and in view of the commercial nature of the partnership business. Pendente lite and future interest are claimed under Section 34 CPC.


CAUSE OF ACTION

13. The cause of action first arose on 01.06.2024 when (i) the partnership firm was dissolved by mutual consent, (ii) the accounts were settled, and (iii) the Defendant acknowledged that Rs. 35,00,000/- is due and payable to the Plaintiff towards the Plaintiff’s share. The cause of action further accrued on {{DEMAND_DATE_1}}, {{DEMAND_DATE_2}} and on each subsequent date when the Plaintiff demanded payment and the Defendant failed/neglected to pay. The cause of action is continuing as the Defendant continues to withhold the admitted amount and the same remains unpaid as on the date of filing of this suit.


LIMITATION

14. The present suit is within limitation. The claim arises from dissolution and settlement on 01.06.2024 and is filed within the prescribed period of limitation (three years from the Date of Dissolution/settlement, as applicable). The Plaintiff is filing the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within the said limitation period. (Specific Limitation Act entry, if required, may be stated as Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}}.)


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

15. The suit is valued for the purposes of court fee at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUATION_FOR_COURT_FEE}} being the principal sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- together with pre-suit interest computed from 01.06.2024 up to {{FILING_DATE}} (as applicable under the court-fees law of the State). The suit is valued for the purposes of jurisdiction at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUATION_FOR_JURISDICTION}}.

16. The requisite court fee is paid/shall be paid as per the applicable Court Fees and Suits Valuation Act and rules and is stated as {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

17. The Plaintiff claims:

(a) Pre-suit interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from 01.06.2024 (Date of Dissolution/settlement) to {{FILING_DATE}};

(b) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree; and

(c) Future interest under Section 34 CPC at such rate (up to 12% per annum as claimed) as this Hon’ble Court deems fit, from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

In the above facts and circumstances, the Plaintiff prays that this Hon’ble Court may be pleased to:

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), being the Plaintiff’s admitted share payable upon dissolution of M/s ABC Enterprises;

(b) Award pre-suit interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from 01.06.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}};

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree;

(d) Award future interest under Section 34 CPC at such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit (up to 12% per annum as claimed) from the date of decree till realization;

(e) Award costs of the suit; and

(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

1. Partnership Deed of M/s ABC Enterprises dated {{PARTNERSHIP_DEED_DATE}} (if any) — Annexure A.
2. Dissolution Deed / Mutual Dissolution Memo / Settlement Record dated 01.06.2024 (if any) — Annexure B.
3. Acknowledgment/Admission by Defendant of amount due of Rs. 35,00,000/- dated {{ACK_DATE}} (if separate) — Annexure C.
4. Any written demand/notice and proof of service, if issued — Annexure D.


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 8 and 13 to 16 are true and correct to my personal knowledge, and the contents of paragraphs 9 to 12 and 17 are based on legal advice which I believe to be true, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate

Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}

blocking_issues.issue: User-requested statutory grounds (Sections 48 and 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932) are not pleaded; the draft instead relies primarily on Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.
blocking_issues.fix: Add a dedicated legal grounds section pleading Section 48 (mode of settlement of accounts after dissolution) and Section 69 (maintainability/registration and applicable exception), while preserving the user-requested Section 65 plea as an alternative/without-prejudice ground.

blocking_issues.issue: Material factual narrative is inconsistent with the USER_REQUEST and introduces new transaction facts (advance payment, performance date, contractual interest clause, bank payment proof) that change the cause of action from ‘share on dissolution with acknowledged balance’ to a ‘loan/advance repayment’ style claim.
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Facts and Interest sections as a chronological dissolution-and-settlement story: dissolution on 01.06.2024, settlement of accounts, acknowledgment of Rs.35,00,000/- payable to Plaintiff, repeated demands, non-payment. Remove/replace invented loan/advance facts and performance-date constructs; keep only placeholders for documents/events that actually exist (e.g., dissolution deed/settlement, acknowledgment, demand notice if any).
blocking_issues.location: FACTS OF THE CASE (paras 1–6) and INTEREST section

blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action / limitation narrative and the Interest section are internally inconsistent: prayer seeks interest from 01.06.2024, but the Interest section claims interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and alleges an advance paid on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} (not pleaded in the user facts).
blocking_issues.fix: Align interest pleading consistently to the user instruction: claim 12% p.a. from 01.06.2024 (date of dissolution/settlement) up to filing (pre-suit), pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 CPC; remove {{DEFAULT_DATE}} / {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} references unless truly applicable.
blocking_issues.location: CAUSE OF ACTION, LIMITATION, and INTEREST sections

non_blocking_issues.issue: List of Documents numbering starts at 7 and uses repeated generic {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} entries, reducing clarity though not fatal if corrected.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Renumber the list from 1 onwards and replace each entry with a specific document description matching the body references (e.g., Partnership Deed as Annexure A; Dissolution/Settlement Deed as Annexure B; Acknowledgment as Annexure C; Demand notice/postal proof only if applicable).

non_blocking_issues.issue: Jurisdiction paragraph includes specific territorial links (bank branch/advance payment/execution) that are not part of the pleaded factual matrix and may be denied; the core links should be tied to partnership business place, dissolution/settlement execution place, and payment being payable within jurisdiction.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Revise jurisdiction pleading to rely on defensible territorial links: Defendant residence/business, firm’s principal place of business, place of dissolution/settlement/acknowledgment, and place where payment was to be made/received.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Limitation paragraph uses placeholders for the Limitation Act article and end-date; while placeholders are acceptable, the pleading can be clearer by stating the applicable period (three years) and anchoring it to 01.06.2024 as pleaded accrual.

final_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
final_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only)
WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN:

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
{{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.  
...Plaintiff

VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
{{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.  
...Defendant


THE PLAINT

1. The Plaintiff is a major and is competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is a major and is competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

2. Territorial Jurisdiction: This Hon’ble Court has territorial jurisdiction since (i) the Defendant resides and/or carries on business within the local limits of this Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, and (ii) the partnership business of M/s ABC Enterprises was carried on at {{FIRM_PRINCIPAL_PLACE_OF_BUSINESS}} within the local limits of this Court, and (iii) the dissolution/settlement between the parties and the Defendant’s acknowledgment of liability were made at {{DISSOLUTION_SETTLEMENT_PLACE}} within the local limits of this Court, and (iv) the amount claimed is payable to the Plaintiff at {{PAYMENT_PLACE}} within the local limits of this Court.

3. Pecuniary Jurisdiction: The suit claim is for recovery of Rs. 35,00,000/- along with interest. The suit valuation for purposes of jurisdiction is stated in the Valuation paragraph below and is within the pecuniary limits of this Hon’ble District Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE (CHRONOLOGICAL)

4. The Plaintiff and the Defendant were partners in a partnership firm in the name and style of “M/s ABC Enterprises”, carrying on the business of construction materials, inter alia at {{FIRM_PRINCIPAL_PLACE_OF_BUSINESS}}. The partnership commenced on {{PARTNERSHIP_START_DATE}}. The partnership deed, if any, is relied upon as Annexure A.

5. Due to mutual differences/for business reasons, the Plaintiff and Defendant mutually agreed to dissolve the partnership firm. Accordingly, the partnership was dissolved by mutual consent on 01.06.2024 (hereinafter “Date of Dissolution”). The dissolution deed/settlement memo, if reduced into writing, is relied upon as Annexure B.

6. Upon dissolution, the partners settled the accounts of the firm and agreed the manner of distribution of the assets and liabilities of the dissolved firm. In such settlement, the Defendant acknowledged that a sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) is due and payable by the Defendant to the Plaintiff towards the Plaintiff’s share in the dissolved partnership firm. The written acknowledgment/settlement record (if separate) is relied upon as Annexure C.

7. After 01.06.2024, the Plaintiff repeatedly demanded payment of the said sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- from the Defendant, including on {{DEMAND_DATE_1}}, {{DEMAND_DATE_2}} and thereafter. Despite repeated demands, the Defendant has failed and neglected to pay the Plaintiff the said sum and continues to wrongfully withhold the Plaintiff’s admitted share.

8. The Defendant’s liability is thus a liquidated sum arising out of the settlement of accounts upon dissolution and the Defendant’s acknowledgment thereof, but remains unpaid till date.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

9. Section 48 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 (user-instructed) sets out the mode of settlement of accounts between partners after dissolution, including adjustment of losses and distribution/application of the assets and surplus among the partners in their respective rights. In the present case, the partnership stood dissolved on 01.06.2024 and the accounts were settled, resulting in an admitted balance of Rs. 35,00,000/- payable to the Plaintiff as his share. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to recover the said admitted sum from the Defendant.

10. Section 69 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 (user-instructed) (a) does not bar the present suit because {{FIRM_REGISTRATION_STATUS_AND_PARTNERS_SHOWN}}. Without prejudice, even otherwise, the Plaintiff’s claim is founded upon settlement of accounts of a dissolved partnership and/or a post-dissolution acknowledgment/arrangement fixing the amount payable, and is maintainable under the applicable exception(s) under the Partnership Act including Section 69(3)(a), as applicable to the facts.

11. Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (user-instructed) is pleaded without prejudice and in the alternative: if this Hon’ble Court holds that the post-dissolution settlement/arrangement recording the Plaintiff’s entitlement is unenforceable/void for any reason, then the Defendant—having retained and continued to retain the monetary benefit representing the Plaintiff’s admitted share—must restore/return the benefit so retained to the Plaintiff.

12. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest. The Plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from 01.06.2024 as specifically instructed, on account of the Defendant’s wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s admitted share after dissolution/settlement, and in view of the commercial nature of the partnership business. Pendente lite and future interest are claimed under Section 34 CPC.


CAUSE OF ACTION

13. The cause of action first arose on 01.06.2024 when (i) the partnership firm was dissolved by mutual consent, (ii) the accounts were settled, and (iii) the Defendant acknowledged that Rs. 35,00,000/- is due and payable to the Plaintiff towards the Plaintiff’s share. The cause of action further accrued on {{DEMAND_DATE_1}}, {{DEMAND_DATE_2}} and on each subsequent date when the Plaintiff demanded payment and the Defendant failed/neglected to pay. The cause of action is continuing as the Defendant continues to withhold the admitted amount and the same remains unpaid as on the date of filing of this suit.


LIMITATION

14. The present suit is within limitation. The claim arises from dissolution and settlement on 01.06.2024 and is filed within the prescribed period of limitation (three years from the Date of Dissolution/settlement, as applicable). The Plaintiff is filing the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within the said limitation period. (Specific Limitation Act entry, if required, may be stated as Article {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}}.)


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

15. The suit is valued for the purposes of court fee at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUATION_FOR_COURT_FEE}} being the principal sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- together with pre-suit interest computed from 01.06.2024 up to {{FILING_DATE}} (as applicable under the court-fees law of the State). The suit is valued for the purposes of jurisdiction at Rs. {{SUIT_VALUATION_FOR_JURISDICTION}}.

16. The requisite court fee is paid/shall be paid as per the applicable Court Fees and Suits Valuation Act and rules and is stated as {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

17. The Plaintiff claims:

(a) Pre-suit interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from 01.06.2024 (Date of Dissolution/settlement) to {{FILING_DATE}};

(b) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree; and

(c) Future interest under Section 34 CPC at such rate (up to 12% per annum as claimed) as this Hon’ble Court deems fit, from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

In the above facts and circumstances, the Plaintiff prays that this Hon’ble Court may be pleased to:

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 35,00,000/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), being the Plaintiff’s admitted share payable upon dissolution of M/s ABC Enterprises;

(b) Award pre-suit interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from 01.06.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}};

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at 12% per annum on Rs. 35,00,000/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree;

(d) Award future interest under Section 34 CPC at such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit (up to 12% per annum as claimed) from the date of decree till realization;

(e) Award costs of the suit; and

(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

1. Partnership Deed of M/s ABC Enterprises dated {{PARTNERSHIP_DEED_DATE}} (if any) — Annexure A.
2. Dissolution Deed / Mutual Dissolution Memo / Settlement Record dated 01.06.2024 (if any) — Annexure B.
3. Acknowledgment/Admission by Defendant of amount due of Rs. 35,00,000/- dated {{ACK_DATE}} (if separate) — Annexure C.
4. Any written demand/notice and proof of service, if issued — Annexure D.


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 8 and 13 to 16 are true and correct to my personal knowledge, and the contents of paragraphs 9 to 12 and 17 are based on legal advice which I believe to be true, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No.: {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}
Date: {{FILING_DATE}}
